full_response:
Let the events of our “strange’’ universe be labelled by the coordinates  

(t , x , y , z) with invariant interval  

Δs² = –Δt² + α² t² Δx² + Δy² + Δz² .  (1)

Notice that the (y , z)-part is already in the usual Minkowski form, so all that is new is the factor α² t² sitting in front of Δx².  
Introduce the following change of variables

T ≡ t cosh(αx) ,  X ≡ t sinh(αx) ,  Y ≡ y ,  Z ≡ z .  (2)

Now compute dT and dX:

dT = cosh(αx) dt + αt sinh(αx) dx ,  
dX = sinh(αx) dt + αt cosh(αx) dx .  (3)

Form the quadratic form of ordinary Minkowski space:

−dT² + dX² = −[cosh(αx) dt + αt sinh(αx) dx]²  
       + [sinh(αx) dt + αt cosh(αx) dx]² .

Expanding and using cosh²θ – sinh²θ = 1, all mixed terms cancel and we obtain

−dT² + dX² = −dt² + α² t² dx² .  (4)

Adding the unchanged transverse pieces gives

−dT² + dX² + dY² + dZ² = −dt² + α² t² dx² + dy² + dz² = ds² .  (5)

Equation (5) shows that the “hypothetical’’ metric (1) is nothing more than the
ordinary Minkowski metric written in the accelerated (Milne–type) coordinates (t , x , y , z).  

Therefore:

• Any result that depends only on the invariant interval (for example, the condition
Δs² = 0 for light rays that you used in part (a), or the expression for a particle’s proper time that you derived in part (b)) must be identical to the corresponding result obtained with the standard Minkowski interval, because (1) and the usual
Δs² = −ΔT² + ΔX² + ΔY² + ΔZ² are mathematically the same object written in
different coordinates.

• Quantities you found in parts (a) and (b) that looked “non–standard’’ are simply the familiar Minkowski quantities expressed in the curvilinear coordinates (t , x , y , z). When they are transformed to the inertial coordinates (T , X , Y , Z) through (2), they reduce exactly to the standard results.

Hence the answers you obtained previously are fully consistent with ordinary special-relativistic physics; the apparent differences arise only from the choice of coordinates, not from any new physics.

\boxed{\text{Parts (a) and (b) agree with the standard invariant distance because the metric }ds^{2}=-dt^{2}+\alpha^{2}t^{2}dx^{2}+dy^{2}+dz^{2}\text{ is related to the Minkowski metric }ds^{2}=-dT^{2}+dX^{2}+dY^{2}+dZ^{2}\text{ by the coordinate transformation }T=t\cosh(\alpha x),\;X=t\sinh(\alpha x).\; } 

Confidence: 55%
